entry.title: Income tax/Direct tax and Tax ledger
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post takes a sides step from Controlling related topics, and focuses on direct tax, however, we still circle around highlighting new functionalities and comparing functionalities between SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (at the time of writing the latest SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition release is 2025, while the earlier mentioned is 2508). 

Typically, in S/4HANA projects you focus on Transactional taxes, like, VAT, e-invoicing mandates, and other tax related topics, which are more “urgent” from operative reporting and compliance point of view. However, direct tax should not be overlooked as there are fundamental building blocks, which can help in automating the process, some which are more costly to implement at a later stage (e.g. adding a tax ledger with Asset Accounting integration in Private Cloud Edition), while others doesn’t matter that when they are implemented (e.g. taking  SAP Profitability and performance management into use).

 

Figure 1: Overview of SAP Tax solutions.

 

You might still wonder, why bother with this, we have managed well earlier also, there are not that big difference between local GAAP and income tax?

  • Well, you have items which are handled differently, e.g. pensions, can be deductible in tax, but not in local GAAP, you can have different depreciation rules for tax, you might have certain fixed and temporary difference between local gap and tax. Furthermore, bad debt and lease related postings can have different handling between local GAAP and direct tax. All that leads to the fact that income tax calculation requires different set of information and different rules to calculate your financials.
  • There are also companies that face a need to automate this process, or want further transparency and auditability, which are also drivers for improving direct tax process.

1.     Direct Tax in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition

To support direct tax calculation in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition you can decide whether you want to have a full ledger (Ledger 3) (ie. a standard, non-leading ledger), have an extension ledger for tax or only to have tax depreciation areas for reporting purposes (without a real tax ledger).

  • A) Standard ledger. The standard ledger approach (ledger 3, in figure 2 below) enables you to have direct tax related asset postings (depreciations and specific adjustments) integrated into a separate ledger. Meaning direct integration from Asset accounting into this ledger. A standard ledger allows also integration from other sub-ledgers, like Revenue Accounting and Lease Accounting or Treasury. This ledger will also include all postings that are done with ledger group SPACE, meaning all standard postings (invoices, material movements, normal closing postings, etc.). Additionally, this ledger would include all the other postings that are only valid for direct tax, which enables you to report the direct tax relevant items from one place (as well as for direct tax audits you have also all postings in one place). Database size should also be considered with this approach, as it will increase.
    • This approach requires also efforts, as you need to do closing activities for this ledger, so more efforts are required to maintain it. Adding a full ledger and asset accounting after go-live is possible, however, there are limitations and requires usually more work (further details: Subsequent Implementation of a Further Accounting Principle SAP HELP & 2403248 - FI-AA (new): Availability of RAFABNEW & 430618 - Procedure for setting up new depreciation area), in this scenario you should also review all the subledgers required for the new accounting principle and not only Asset Accounting. Subsequent addition of an accounting principle is not yet possible if Universal Parallel Accounting (UPA) is activated.
  • B) Extension ledger.  Having an extension ledger (e.g. on top of your local ledger (Ledger 2 in below example) enables you to make manual postings into the ledger, to have all direct tax adjustments in one ledger (reported together with the underlying ledger). An extension ledger supports only manual postings to balance sheet and P&L accounts. No postings to reconciliation accounts or other automated postings are supported. This means that no manual postings to subledgers, vendor, customer, asset or material can be done there. An extension ledger can be added after go-live. This approach is not recommended approach, as reporting is very limited, e.g. asset accounting (or any other sub-ledger) cannot read from this ledger.
  • C) No additional ledger. Some customers opt for not having any ledger at all for direct tax and only having a statistical tax depreciation area (to follow and report tax depreciation). This means you need to track pure direct tax related postings and reporting outside your SAP system.

 

 

Figure 2: Ledger options with SAP S/4 HANA Private Cloud Edition.

Corporate Income Tax Framework is also available, however, the  country scope is limited and is not planned to be extended, so usually not very commonly discussed.

 

2.     Direct Tax in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition you can have statistical depreciation areas for tax and an extension ledger, but it is currently not possible to have full ledger for tax. This means options B and C from previous chapter are valid approaches.

  • B) Extension ledger. Having an extension ledger (on top of your local ledger (Ledger 2, in below example) enables you to make manual postings into the ledger, to have all direct tax adjustments in one ledger (reported together with the underlying ledger). An extension ledger supports only manual postings to balance sheet and P&L accounts. No postings to reconciliation accounts or other automated postings are supported. This means that no manual postings to subledgers, vendor, customer, asset or material can be done there.
    • An extension ledger can be added after go-live. This approach is not recommended approach, as reporting is very limited, e.g. asset accounting (or any other sub-ledger) cannot read from this ledger.
  • C) No additional ledger. Some customers opt for not having any ledger at all for direct tax and only having a statistical tax depreciation area (to follow and report tax depreciation). This means you need to track pure direct tax related postings and reporting outside your SAP system. SAP pre-delivers per country depreciation areas, which typically includes also a tax depreciation area, but there are country specific differences.
    • Note! Currently only one customer specific (statistical) depreciation area(area 16)  can be added (for local GAAP), during initial implementation, not after go-live (scope item 5HG). So, if you need an additional depreciation area, it can only be added before go-live (no asset postings can exist when you add it). In some cases, you might also have requirements for an additional depreciation area for insurance follow-up (not direct tax). This means it is extra important to check you depreciation areas in the implementation phase. 

 

 

Figure 3: Ledger options in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud Edition.

The Corporate Income Tax Framework , is also available, but also with limited country scope and is not planned to be extended, so usually not very commonly discussed.

 

3.     Profitability and Performance Management, taking automation to the next level

So far, we have discussed ledgers and asset accounting, but what about all the other posting differences, how to automate them? This is where SAP Profitability & Performance management (PaPM) comes into picture.

PaPM is a strong calculation engine, which was designed to be maintained by business users and not require too much IT support. It has multiple use cases, one of which is corporate income tax. It brings automation, transparency, auditability and process overview to the direct income tax process.  PaPM enables also AI-assisted tax analysis and insights through integration with the Joule copilot.

Using the underlying data discussed in previous chapters, you can complete the remaining calculations and adjustments in PaPM. This enables to have a systematic and rule-based approach for temporary and permanent adjustment, as well as other adjustments. Optionally, PaPM can also make postings back to the tax ledger (or extension ledger (within its limitations)) (both for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition), for a single source of truth of direct tax postings. Technically PaPM can integrate to any source (also non-SAP), so you can also write back data to SAP ECC.

 

Figure 4: PaPM example with integration to Full tax ledger, together with PaPm and S/4 HANA Private Cloud edition.

To speed up a PaPM implementation you can use the SAP created sample content (SAP PaPM Sample Content All), for further information on the  SAP PaPM Tax Sample content, click here.  In addition to SAP sample content, there are multiple tax partners who create and maintain even more detailed sample content.

 

Figure 5: Overview of Corporate income tax with SAP.

 

It is worth noticing that PaPm comes with multiples other use cases also, BEPS pillar 2, Transfer pricing, Sustainability, Agile planning & Simulations, Activity based costing, etc.

 

Figure 6: Overview of PaPm use cases.

 

4.     Summary

This blog covered different options on how to handle direct tax, whether you only have partial support for direct tax in S/4HANA e.g. from statistical tax depreciation areas in asset accounting or having a full tax ledger, with PaPM you can gain further automation, auditability and process transparency. There are different options, depending on what type of setup suites your organization.  

 

Figure 7: Comparison Table: Direct Tax Management in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, Public Cloud, and PaPM.

 

  • How automated is your direct tax processes, are you fully automated and auditable or are you using excel?

 

Best Regards,

Dick Westman

entry.title: Execute SAP PaPM UM API via SAC through Multi Actions’ API step
entry.content:
Introduction

User can trigger SAP PaPM Universal Model API via SAC Multi Actions’ API Step, here is the step by step blog to set up such a workflow.

Create Multi Action with API Step to trigger SAP PaPM UM API

  1. Create HTTP API Connection
    1. Choose Connections on SAC menu
    2. Choose Add Connections
    3. Search “HTTP API”
    4. Fill in the required connection information, then choose Create.

  1. Create Multi Action and add API step connecting to the public API of SAP PaPM UM

  1. Execute Multi Action
    1. This multi action can be executed in story, analytics application, and scheduled via Calendar. Here is the example of running in Story.
    2. Add Multi Action Starter.
    3. Choose this Multi Action on the builder panel.
    4. Execute this Multi Action.

  1. Check calculation result on SAP PaPM UM
    1. The PaPM UM process is generated.
    2. Choose “Show Data” to check the calculation result on TOTAL column.
    3. The result of TOTAL is correct.
      • The value of “QUANTITY” we set in Multi Actions’ API Step is “2”, according to the calculation rule “TOTAL = AMOUNT*QUANTITY”, the formula is “AMOUNT*2”, so the result on TOTAL is correct.

Summary

With Multi Actions API step, user can execute SAP PaPM UM API via SAC. What's more, Multi Actions can be parameterized which largely reduces maintenance effort for admin users.  Business users execute multi action with defined context and scope by assigning parameter values flexibly. 

You can find more details in the blog of Multi Actions API Step. Integrate remote application via API step of Multi Actions

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-01-19
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community.

Happy New Year and welcome to 2026! We’re excited to kick things off by highlighting the features released in late Q4 2025. Join me as I walk you through the newest features and improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM).

To stay up to date with previous blog posts from my colleagues, you can always go through and read them all using this tag PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew .

With that, let’s begin.

Model

Model Transfer Structure

A new function type has been introduced to the Model Transfer Structure which offers the types:

  1. Transfer Structure 
  2. Reverse Transfer Structure 

It has the same functional capabilities as Transfer Structure, but the only difference is there is no need to Run the function since it is on-the-fly wherein Environment activation is enough.  

In Create dialog on Manage Functions application, the new function type Model Transfer Structure is displayed in Type field dropdown. 

In Model Transfer Structure configuration page, two types are displayed which are Transfer Structure and Reverse Transfer Structure in Type dropdown field under General Information section. 

In Universal Model Homepage, the new tile Manage Model Transfer Structures for the new function type is displayed under Model tab.  

For more information, please refer to Application Help for Universal Model Model Transfer Structure 

Currency and Unit Conversions

We’ve introduced a new enhancement across the Manage Currency Conversions, Manage Model Currency Conversions, Manage Unit Conversions, and Manage Model Unit Conversions applications to make configuration simpler and more user-friendly. With this update, the Target Value and Target Unit field/parameter value help now displays only the units mapped to the selected Conversion Input Function, instead of showing all units created in the environment.

This reduces confusion and makes it easier for users to quickly identify and maintain the correct fields needed for their conversion function setup.

Fields maintained in the Input Functions for both Currency Conversion and Model Currency Conversion. 

As shown below, the fields in the Target Value and Target Unit field/parameter display only the units mapped to the selected Currency Conversion Input Function.

This behavior is the same for Unit Conversion and Model Unit Conversion. Only the field mapped to the selected Conversion Input Function are displayed in the Target Value and Target Unit field/parameter.

Report

Manage Reports

Integrate Perplexity Web Search into Report Copilot

This feature allows you to use the Web Search tool to get up-to-date information directly from the web, making your reports more accurate, reliable, and informative.

SAP Joule serves as an AI copilot embedded in SAP applications, including report generation in tools like SAP PaPM Cloud UM, to deliver real-time insights grounded in business data. Integrating Perplexity's Sonar and Sonar Pro models as optional web search providers elevates this capability by enabling dynamic external data retrieval, superior citations, and contextual enrichment for workflows such as basic/advanced report generation, rewrites, section additions, highlighted updates, and general queries.

The enhancement adds Perplexity options in "Manage Connections" and "Manage Tenant Connections," including toggles for "Enable Web Search" (making model selection mandatory when active) and dropdowns for Sonar or Sonar Pro.

How It Works: 

The fields below become mandatory when you mark the Enable Web Search feature. You can select one of the following web search models for your report: 

  • “Sonar”: Select this option to perform real-time web searches and access a wide range of online information. 
  • “Sonar Pro”: Select this option for an advanced search and detailed analysis. This enhanced version of “Sonar” is designed for in-depth search and detailed insights. 

Highlight Changes in Report Versions: 

This is an additional feature for the Copilot that allows you to choose a specific section of text in the report and send a prompt to update it. It reviews the surrounding context to ensure that the changes fit naturally with the rest of the report. After the update, you can review the changes made: the system highlights what has been added or removed, and you can choose to accept or reject each change individually or apply all changes at once. 

"Report Copilot's New Tracked Changes: Green for Adds, Red for Removes – Your Workflow Just Got Better" 

Key Features: 

  • Visual Highlights: Added content shows with green underlines; deletions appear as red strikethroughs for instant recognition. 
  • Granular Controls: Accept or reject individual changes, or use bulk Accept All/Reject All buttons. 
  • Undo Integration: Undo reverts to prior states with accept/reject options intact, syncing seamlessly across features. 
  • Full Compatibility: Works with all Report Copilot actions, ensuring the final report reflects only accepted changes. 

How It Works: 

  • Make edits via write, rewrite, etc., and changes track automatically. 
  • Click per-change buttons or bulk options to accept/reject. 

  • View highlights inline in the editor. 
  • Hit undo to restore previous tracked state.​ 

  • Include screenshots of green/red highlights and buttons for visual appeal.

Show Screen

Show Screen: Column Label Sorting in Pivot Mode 

We’re making pivot mode more powerful and user-friendly! You can now sort data by fields assigned to Column Labels—just like you already do with Row Groups. 

Users working in Pivot Mode within the Show Screen can now apply sorting directly to Column Label fields using dedicated sort icons: 

  • Ascending 
  • Descending 

This works for any field placed under the Column Labels section. 

How It Works 

  • Click on a column name under Column Labels to apply sorting. 
  • Sorting behavior mimics Row Group sorting - data is reordered according to the selected “Sort by” field.

This enhancement gives users greater flexibility when analyzing pivoted data and improves consistency between Row Groups and Column Labels. 


That’s all for now!

We hope these new features and enhancements make your experience with Cloud UM even better. Stay tuned for more “What’s New” updates—published every month.

Share this blogpost with your colleagues and friends. Thank you!

entry.title: Using FileAdapter in SAP HANA SDI for SAP PaPM Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community! Welcome to this blogpost on how to use FileAdapter in SAP HANA Smart Data Integration (SDI) for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud integration. 

In our earlier blog on SAP PaPM Cloud Integration with Microsoft Excel, we explored how ExcelAdapter can help business users to bring their Excel file data directly into the SAP PaPM Cloud.

But what happens when your data is not in an Excel file? What if your data is coming in the form of *.csv, *.txt or a tab-delimited file? How can we consume the data in our SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud?

This is where HANA SDI’s FileAdapter comes in.

Before walking through the steps on how to set up FileAdapter for SAP PaPM Cloud Inbound Integration. Here's a quick comparison between FileAdapter and ExcelAdapter:

Comparison

FileAdapter

ExcelAdapter

File Type

*.csv, *.txt, logs

*.xls, *.xlsx

Structure

Delimited (commas, tabs, pipes) 

Tabular, sheet-based

Best For

Raw exports, transactional data

Spreadsheet, planning models

Special Features

Pattern-based reading, real-time replication

Sheet/range selection, metadata handling

Complexity

Requires config file (.cfg) for parsing

More intuitive if you're used to Excel

 

Prerequisites:

  • SAP HANA Data Provisioning Agent is installed and connected to SAP PaPM Cloud. See Data Provisioning Agent Installation and Update Guide and Configuration Guide for SAP HANA Cloud.
  • Register Data Provisioning Adapter for FileAdapter. See Register Data Provisioning Adapters
  • Prepare your source file in a *.csv or *.txt file. Make sure it has headers (column names) and consistent delimiters (commas, tabs or pipes)

Step 1: Create Configuration File in the Data Provisioning Agent

Open a command prompt as Administrator and navigate to <DPAgent_DIR>/agentutils directory. Execute the command to create a configuration file (*.cfg) to tell FileAdapter how to interpret your source file. See Generate the Configuration File with a Command-Line Utility.

createfileformat.bat -file "<CSV folder path>\filename.csv" -firstRowAsColumnName true -cfgdir "<CSV folder path>"

 

Step 2: Prepare Configuration File

The first line in your Configuration File should always include a comment or a description. For further guidance, see File Format Configuration Files

 

Step 3: Configure FileAdapter Preference

Next step is to authenticate the file using AccessToken. To retrieve the AccessToken, open a Command Prompt and enter command: whoami /user. Make sure to record the Security Identifier (SID) as this will be required later in the FileAdapter Preference configuration.

Once we have the SID information, open a Command Prompt as Administrator and go to <DPAgent_Dir>/bin and enter command: agentcli.bat --configAdapters. When prompted, select Option 2 (Set Adapter Preference) then proceed to choose Option 7 (Set FileAdapter Preference). Provide the following information:

Fields

Value

Root Directory

No remote source can reach beyond this directory for data files. This means that you must use this folder/subfolder for remote source configuration

File Format For Root Directory

No remote source can reach beyond this directory for data files. This means that you must use this folder/subfolder for remote source configuration

PGP Files (Secret Key and Thirdparty Public Key) Root DirectoryEnter the folder where PGP files (secret key and thirdparty public key) is located. If these files are not available, you may simply use the Root Directory as the location.

Access Token

An access token protects the files from access from different agents. Use this password when creating a remote source.

 

Step 4: Set up Remote Source Connection in the SAP HANA Cloud

Go to the HANA Database Explorer of the PaPM Cloud application and set up a Remote Source Connection. Enter the specified values in the corresponding fields.

FIELDS

VALUE

Source Name

Provide source name for the remote connection

Adapter Name

FileAdapter

Source Location

Select the name of the registered DPAgent

Source Option

Local File System

Target Option

Local File System

Root Directory

<CSV Folder Location>

Directory of the file format definitial

<CSV Folder Location>

Use Decryption for Reading

None

Credentials Mode

Technical User

Password

Enter the Security Identifier (SID)

 

Step 5: Map the File to Virtual Table

Create Virtual Table for the file by selecting the remote source connection and locating the corresponding table for the CSV file, and then click Create Virtual Object(s) to generate the Virtual Table.

Create a name for the virtual table and choose the Schema where it will be store, and then, click Create.

 

Open the content of the Virtual Table to see the contents. Note that in the contents additional columns (PATH, NAME, ROWNUMBER, ERROR and PARTITION) are automatically generated.

 

Step 6: Consume in SAP PaPM Cloud

In the SAP PaPM Cloud, you can now proceed to use the virtual table as an input function and your SAP PaPM Cloud can now pull in data directly from the file source.

Standard Model

Refer to Accessing Artifacts from External SAP HANA Cloud Schema to make your schema accessible as an authoring schema in HANA views or table connections in SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model.

Universal Model

Refer to Accessing Artifacts from External SAP HANA Cloud Schema to make your schema accessible as an authoring schema in HANA views or table connections in SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model.

Also, for Universal Model, when creating a connection of type “HANA Schema”, you may encounter the following error:

This error happens because the ROWNUMBER field (automatically generated when using FileAdapter) cannot be used as a valid key field in SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model. SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model requires key fields to be either UUID or String type. Since the ROWNUMBER field is of type Integer64, SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model fails to sync.

To successfully activate the Environment when using FileAdapter in SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model, you need to complete the following steps:

1. In the Environment, add a UUIDME field as a characteristic with type UUID

2. Under Connection, replace the Model Field ROWNUMBER to UUIDME. Then, click Apply to save your changes. 

 3. Perform Model Sync and Connection Sync. Then, proceed to activate your environment.

4. Once the environment is activated successfully, you may proceed to check the HANA schema and show the data without any issues.

And that's how you can use FileAdapter in SAP HANA SDI for your SAP PaPM Cloud integration! Thank you for sticking with me on this walkthrough! Try it out in your own environments and let us know how it goes. Share your experiences, tips, or questions in the comments section. Let's keep learning and growing together!

entry.title: Can ExcelAdapter Handle Custom Path in SAP PaPM Cloud integration?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!😊

In the earlier SAP Community blogpost SAP PaPM Cloud Integration with Microsoft Excel, we looked at how SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud leverages HANA Smart Data Integration (SDI) with the ExcelAdapter to consume Excel files.

By default, ExcelAdapter only looks for files in the <DPAGENT_DIR>/excel folder. This setup is OK most of the time but what happens when the business wants to make some changes in the location of their Excel files… may it be because of the main drive running out of storage space or reorganizing the folder structure or a cleaner setup. Suddenly, the setup feels more like a limitation than a convenience.

And here’s the catch: if you try to point the Remote Source Configuration to a custom folder, you’ll hit an error just like in the screenshot below.

Does that mean you're stuck on using the default directory? Not quite because we have a workaround. The trick is to use a symbolic link (symlink). In this workaround, the ExcelAdapter will think that it will pull the Excel files from <DPAGENT_DIR>/excel, but in reality, your files are located in other directory.

Let's break it down on how we can achieve this:

Prerequisites:

  • You have an Administrator access in the OS
  • Ensure that the DP Agent Service User have access to the directory to avoid any issue as per SAP Note 3303503

For Windows:

1. Open command prompt as Administrator using the DP Agent Service User and go to <DPAGENT_DIR> directory

2. Enter command: mklink /D excel <Target Directory where your Excel Files are Stored>

For Linux/Unix:

Use the ln-s command: 

ln -s <Target Directory where Excel Files are Stored> <DPAGENT_DIR>/excel

This workaround gives you the flexibility to scale storage, keep things tidy, and adapt to changing business needs—all while staying within ExcelAdapter’s rules.

Have you tried this workaround yourself? Share your story in the comments. 😊

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What’s New as of 2026-02-02?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community and Happy New Year! 🎉

Welcome to the first 2026 edition of the “What’s New” series for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). As we kick off the year, we’re excited to share a few updates focused on strengthening operational visibility, improving the user experience, and supporting smoother collaboration across teams.

Let’s dive into what’s new! 😊

User Settings | User Profile

Great news! We’re introducing a new User Profile entry point in the application header to make user-related actions easier to find and faster to access. This enhancement is designed for signed-in users and provides a centralized place to review account details, adjust display preferences, manage notifications, and quickly access application information

With this update, users can open the User Profile (top-right) and access three options:

  • User Preferences
  • About
  • Sign Out

Previously, these options were accessible from the hamburger menu (left-side navigation). They are now moved to the User Profile (top-right) to keep navigation focused on application areas, while user actions remain consistently available in the header.

Choosing User Preferences opens a dedicated dialog titled “User Preferences”, with three sections on the left for quick navigation:

  • Account

  • Language and Format

  • Notifications

This keeps frequently used personalization settings in one place and reduces the need to navigate across different areas of the UI.

The About option opens a dialog titled “About This Application”

Choosing Sign Out logs the user out of the application.

To learn more about User Profile, please refer to the User Profile | SAP Help Portal

Settings Screen | Destination Settings: SAP Cloud ALM

A new enhancement has been introduced in the Destinations Settings section under the SAP Cloud ALM option. When changes are made to the SAP Cloud ALM destination configuration and saved, an exception log is automatically sent to SAP Cloud ALM within the Integration and Exception Monitoring. This ensures full traceability of the changes, faster troubleshooting, and improved audit trail compliance for destination changes.

Modeling | Runtime Attributes (Stored Procedure Viewer)

When a modeler activates a function, SAP PaPM generates runtime artifacts (such as tables and stored procedures) that support execution.

In some cases, activation can fail, and the stored procedure may not be available in the database making it harder to understand what went wrong.

With this enhancement, modelers can now view the generated stored procedure source code directly in the Modeling UI under Runtime Attributes, helping them troubleshoot and identify modeling issues more easily even when activation is not successful.

What’s improved

  • More self-service troubleshooting: Quickly check the generated procedure text to understand activation problems and adjust the model accordingly.
  • On-demand loading: The procedure source is fetched only when the modeler opens/views it, keeping the Modeling screen responsive.
  • Secure approach: Designed to support debugging without exposing procedure source code through general logging for broader audiences.

Where it’s available

This viewer is available under Runtime Attributes for these function types:

  • Allocation
  • Calculation
  • Conversion
  • Derivation
  • Transfer Structure
  • Join
  • View

How to use it

  1. Go to Modeling and select a supported function
  2. Activate the supported function if not yet activated
  3. In the Properties Panel, choose the drop-down arrow of Runtime Attributes.
  4. Expand Stored Procedure to load and display the stored procedure text

Successful activation:

Failed activation:

Teams | In-App Notification

An enhancement for notifications in the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model is now available.  If the user has notifications enabled, they will receive the new team-related notifications by email, in-app, or both.

Users will be alerted in the following scenarios:

  • When they are assigned to or unassigned from a team
  • When their team is added or removed as a child team
  • When their team’s name or description is updated
  • When their team becomes obsolete, or is restored from an obsolete status
  • When their team is deleted

See sample image below:

To learn more about notification settings, please refer to the Notifications | SAP Help Portal

What’s New on SAP Help Portal

We’re excited to share that SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud now has a dedicated What’s New section on the SAP Help Portal. This new page complements the existing What’s New blog posts in the SAP Community and provides a central, structured view of all product updates.

The What’s New page offers a clear overview of the latest features, enhancements, and content updates across the Standard Model, Universal Model, and Sample Content. Updated in alignment with the product delivery schedule, it gives customers a simple, reliable way to stay informed about ongoing improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud.

How to View Standard Model–Specific Updates

To display only Standard Model–related information in the What’s New table, follow these steps:

  1. Open the What's New to SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud page on the SAP Help Portal.
  2. Under Release Type, select one of the following options:
    1. Standard Model – shows updates for the default release cadence mode
    2. Standard Model Fast Track – shows updates for the Fast Track cadence mode
  3. (Optional) Under Scope, refine your view:
    1. Application – updates relevant for all users
    2. Sample Content – updates related to Sample Content
    3. Administration – updates relevant for administrators
  4. (Optional) Under Type, filter by New, Changed, Deleted or Deprecated.
  5. (Optional) Under Available as Of, choose a specific date range to explore updates for a particular time period.

That’s a wrap! 🎁

Thank you for reading, and we hope these updates help make your day-to-day work in SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model even smoother.

Stay tuned for the next edition of our “What’s New” series as we continue to roll out more enhancements throughout 2026.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-02-03
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community, 

As we move into February, it’s time to explore the latest updates and enhancements delivered during January 2026. In this edition of  What's New, I’ll walk you through the latest features and improvements delivered in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM).  

To stay up to date with previous blog posts from my colleagues, you can always go through and read them all using PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag. 

Let’s take a closer look at what’s new in February. 

Report  

Drag-and-Drop Reordering of Inserted Tabs in Reports 

You can now reorder inserted tabs within the Report page editor. 

This enhancement allows you to reorder inserted tabs using drag-and-drop directly within the Report screen. It improves flexibility and usability by enabling tab order adjustments without removing and reinserting tabs. This works directly within the Report screen once the tabs have been added. 

To reorder tabs, switch the Report Page to Edit mode. After inserting tabs, simply drag and drop them within the page editor to arrange them in the desired order. 

Home Screen Applications References in Reports 

You can now link Home Screen apps to Report Elements. 

A new button is available under Set Reference functionality in Reports, providing a drop-down list of Universal Model home page applications. This allows you to select and link an app in the same way as already existing references. 

This enhancement provides more flexibility to customize reports and navigation by directly connecting report elements with the relevant application from the home page application 

To create a link, switch the Report Page to Edit mode, hover over the grid, and select the Set Reference icon. 

In the Set Reference configuration window, open the Other tab. Choose Apps, and then select the required Home Screen app from the drop-down list. 

Data Screen 

Export numeric values to Excel without currency symbols 

You can now export report data to Excel with numeric values only, without currency symbols. 

In addition to the existing right-click export, a new export option Export to Excel – Numeric Values Only is available. When selected, amounts containing currency information in the report are exported as pure numeric values, allowing Excel to recognize them as numbers for easy validation, calculation and further processing without manual cleanup. 

The existing export functionality remains unchanged and continues to export values together with currency information. 

This is also applicable to export from Show Elements in Reports.  

Consistent column formatting in drill-down views 

As you already know, you can explore the detailed records behind aggregated key figures directly in the Data screen - in Pivot mode key figures are displayed as clickable links. When you select a key figure, a second list view (drill-down detailed view) appears at the bottom of the screen, showing the detailed data records that contribute to the selected value.  

From now on, column formatting defined in pivot tables is applied to drill-down detailed views in the Data screen.  

When you drill down to detailed records, the system inherits the column formatting settings from the originating pivot view. This includes display options such as “Text only”, “Value only”, “Text Last” and “Text First”. 

As a result, detailed records are displayed consistently with the aggregated pivot view, improving readability, and ensuring a uniform data presentation across different levels of analysis. 

To change the formatting for Grouped Row Data, hover over the Group field to see the menu where you can choose the required Text Arrangement option. 

To change the formatting for the Column labels, proceed as follows: 

  1. Choose the Column pane in the sidebar element and choose Edit (pencil icon) next to the field name.
  2. In the popup window that appears select required Text Arrangement from drop-down list with options.
  3. Choose Save or Cancel to discard changes. 
  4. Choose Refresh to apply the changes. 

After changing the formatting, the drill-down view must be re-applied by selecting the number for which you want to display details. 

This is also applicable to drill-down views in Show Elements in Reports. 

Dynamic hide and read-only conditions at cell level across Data Screen views 

You can now configure dynamic conditions in Adapt UI to control the visibility and editability of fields at cell level, with consistent behavior across both the Object Page and the Data screen view. 

Conditions defined in Adapt UI allow you to hide or show specific field values and to switch between editable and read-only states. The same logic is applied consistently to: 

  • fields on the Object Page, and  
  • corresponding cells in the Data screen. 

 When the defined conditions are not met, fields remain visible and editable in both views. All hide and read-only rules are saved as part of the Adapt UI configuration and persist after reload or re-login. 

You can base hide and read-only conditions on values of other fields within the same row, and the behavior updates after refresh when those driver values change. 

You can also remove or disable the configured conditions to restore the default state, where fields are fully visible and editable. 

Once defined for one record, the rules/conditions are applied across the entire dataset. 

Hidden Conditions  

Read-Only Conditions  

What’s New on SAP Help Portal 

We’re excited to announce that SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud now features a dedicated What’s New section on the SAP Help Portal. This new page complements the existing What’s New blog posts in the SAP Community and provides a centralized, structured overview of all product updates. 
The What’s New page offers a clear and comprehensive view of the latest features, enhancements, and content updates across the Standard Model, Universal Model, and Sample Content. Updated in line with the product delivery schedule, it gives customers a simple and reliable way to stay informed about ongoing improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud. 

Proceed as follows: 

  1. Open the What’s New to SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud  page on the SAP Help Portal. 
  2. Under Release Type, select Universal Model to display only updates related to the Universal Model application.
  3. (Optional) Use the Scope filter to further refine the results: Application – updates relevant for all users; Sample Content – updates related to sample content; Administration – updates relevant for administrators.
  4.  (Optional) Under Type, filter the updates by New, Changed, Deleted, or Deprecated.
  5. (Optional) Use the Available as Of filter to select a specific date range and explore updates from a specific time period. 

Using these filters helps you quickly identify the Universal Model changes that are most relevant to your work and stay informed efficiently. 

 That’s it for this update! 

We hope these new features and improvements help you get even more value from SAP PaPM Cloud UM. Don’t miss our upcoming What’s New editions—we share fresh updates every month. 

Feel free to share this blog post with your colleagues and peers. Thank you for reading!

entry.title: CPE-UM Environment Activation Remains ‘Is Finished = No’ – How to Identify and Resolve the Issue
entry.content:
In the PaPM Cloud Universal Model, users frequently face an issue where the CPE–UM environment activation remains in the ‘Is Finished’ state as ‘No’ indefinitely, and no error logs are recorded for the Activate event.

In such cases, the environment activation does not end in either ‘Error’ or ‘Yes’ status. Instead, it remains ‘No’ forever after activation is triggered. In some scenarios, the environment activation ends with an error, but the error message itself is not meaningful. It does not clearly indicate the root cause or specify which function is causing the issue, making it difficult to identify configuration bugs.

In PaPM On-Premise, the system clearly highlights the function with errors using an error icon on the individual function. However, in the Universal Model, no such indication is available. Only the Runtime Status of a function is displayed as Current, Not Current, or Inactive, which is not sufficient to identify configuration issues or bugs.

Due to this limitation, users often end up raising an OSS ticket to resolve the issue.

However, this issue can often be resolved internally by using the Validate option available in the Manage Environment tile. The Validate option checks the entire environment configuration for inconsistencies and clearly reports (if any errors):

  1. Detailed error messages
  2. The affected Function ID(s)
  3. Relevant information in the event logs


For environments with a large number of functions and complex calculations, it is strongly recommended to validate the environment before activation. This helps identify configuration issues early and saves significant time and effort that would otherwise be spent troubleshooting activation failures.

Using the Validate option before activating the environment helps ensure configuration consistency and avoids unnecessary confusion during activation.

Conclusion
When a CPE–UM environment activation remains in Is Finished = No, it becomes difficult to identify the root cause due to limited error details. Using the Validate option in the Manage Environment tile helps detect configuration issues and provides clear error information before activation. Validating the environment in advance can save time and avoid unnecessary troubleshooting.

Thank you for reading this blog post.
If there are any questions or doubts, please feel free to leave a comment. I’ll be happy to help. Have a great day!

entry.title: Filters Not Applied as Expected When Using Model Functions in PaPM Cloud Universal Model
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

In PaPM on-prem, the modeler knows that the package selections used in process templates are applied as filters during function runtime execution. Filter values maintained in the package selection fields are dynamically applied as filters in the modeling setup.

For instance, if a processing function is executed using a process selection field with Quarter = ‘Q3’, and the field Quarter is present in the modeling setup, the value provided is automatically applied as a filter across the overall modeling setup during execution without requiring any additional complex selection filter configuration. This reduces the processing of irrelevant records during function execution and calculation. As a result, modeling performance improves significantly, leading to faster execution times.

Coming to PaPM Cloud Universal Model, UM supports modeling setups using both normal functions (that is, non-model functions such as Calculation, Join, View, etc.) and Model Functions.

In PaPM Cloud Universal Model, I observed unexpected behavior when using Model Functions as part of the modeling setup. When Model Functions are used as the core of the modeling setup, the package selection filters used in the process do not work as expected.

The expected behavior is that filters should be applied prior to the calculation during function execution. However, the actual behavior is that the filters are applied only after the calculation, not before.

Let me explain this behavior using a simple business example for better understanding.

  • Modeling Setup 1 – Expected Behavior
  • Modeling Setup 2 – Actual Behavior

The business example is to calculate the Total Discount Amount for High-Value Orders. Two tables are used: CustomersModelEntity and OrdersModelEntity.

 

 

 

The goal is to calculate the total discount amount for a selected customerGroup and a defined orderAmount range. In this example, a static 10% discount is used in the formula to calculate the discountedOrderAmount.

Modeling Setup 1 – Expected Behavior (without using Model Functions)

To calculate the discountedOrderAmount for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range during processing, a calculation function is used.

This calculation function calculates the totalDiscount amount for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range during processing.

The function is executed using activities created for the writer function along with package selections where:

  • customerGroup = ‘Retail’
  • orderAmount between 10,000 and 50,000

Below is the expected result screenshot after successful execution using the package selection fields.

Modeling Setup 2 – Actual Behavior (when using Model Functions)

The below screenshot shows the Model Calculation function, which is used to calculate the discountedOrderAmount using the same formula as in the expected behavior modeling setup for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range.

The Model Calculation function is then used to calculate the totalDiscount amount using the same formula as in the expected behavior modeling setup.

The function is executed using activities created for the writer function along with package selections where:

  • customerGroup = ‘Retail’
  • orderAmount between 10,000 and 50,000

Below is the actual result screenshot after successful execution using the package selection fields.

Here, the expected result is 4,000, which is the correct aggregation of the discounted amount for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range. However, in Modeling Setup 2, what actually happens is that the calculation defined in the Model Functions is executed first, and only in the final result are the filter values from the activity selection applied.

As a result, the value 6,800 is shown, which represents the aggregation of all discounted amounts irrespective of customer group and other filters.

I am wondering whether this is the intended behavior of Model Functions, or if something needs to be enhanced or fixed in the Universal Model component. If any SAP UM experts read this blog, please share your thoughts.

Conclusion

When using Model Functions in the modeling setup, filters are not properly applied during function execution. The filters are applied only after the calculation, which leads to incorrect results. To avoid such issues, the recommended approach is to use normal functions instead of Model Functions for processing.

If anyone has faced a similar challenge, please share the workaround you have used. Also, feel free to leave a comment if you have any questions or suggestions.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What’s New as of 2026-03-02
entry.content:
Joule | AI Enhancements


Several improvements have been introduced to make working with Joule more flexible, intuitive, and user-friendly, with a strong focus on giving users more control.

What’s improved:


• Use Joule Without Saving a Report: You can now start using Joule immediately without first saving a report, allowing you to gain insights faster directly from your current context.

• Ask Joule About Charts: Joule can now understand the data behind charts, allowing you to ask questions about trends, comparisons, or calculated values shown in a visualization.

• Stop an Ongoing Joule Response: Users can now interrupt Joule while it is generating and streaming a response, providing more control over the interaction.


• Reasoning AI Model: From the Settings screen, you can now select a specific Reasoning AI Model for complex questions, while the Default AI Model handles faster tasks like chat title generation.


For an overview of supported and recommended AI models, see Supported Foundation Models.

 

Process Management | Download and Upload of Process Instances


We have enhanced the XLSX download and upload experience to improve operational efficiency for scenarios like daily closing chains.


• Consolidated XLSX Download: Downloading multiple selected process instances now produces one consolidated XLSX file instead of a ZIP containing multiple files.

• Source Process Mapping: The downloaded file includes a new “Source Process” column that stores the originating process ID for each instance.


• Flexible Uploads: The Upload action is now available even when no process is selected. If the XLSX contains the “Source Process” column, the system creates instances based on that mapping.


Why it matters


This improves operational efficiency for scenarios like daily closing chains by reducing repetitive uploads, improving consistency across templates, and lowering the chance of format-related errors.

For more information about this update, see Process Management.

 

Modeling Screen | Processing Functions


With the latest update, the new modeling function design is now the standard UI. Previously offered as an optional feature, this design moves the Input, Lookup, Signature, Output, and Checks node to the properties panel allowing you to view and update all function settings in one place.

 

 

For more information, see Function Building Blocks.

Message Logs


We also have enhanced the message logs from modeling and other areas of the application. Now, when the message log shows more than 50 lines from singular, multiple, or whole environment activation or run, then a redirection to Application Monitor will be shown.


We aim to make sure the specific Run ID is focused to ensure that users will be able to see all relevant messages from a particular action and avoid missing an important line.

 

RFA | Improved Logging Visibility


We’ve improved RFA logging visibility by enriching the results (Y-table) with additional posting messages on top of the existing Application Monitor updates.

Header-level Outcomes: Technical messages like “Document posted successfully” are now written into the technical field FS_PER_MSG_TEXT_.

 

 

Easier Tracing: This enhancement makes it easier to trace posting feedback directly on the Data Management screen (with Show technical fields enabled) without relying solely on application logs.


Built-In Help and Notifications

 

SAP Companion in Visualize: The Built-In Help, also known as SAP Companion, is now accessible within the Visualize screen to provide guided information and detailed descriptions to enhance your user experience.


Expanded Notifications: You can now receive both in-app and email notifications when a scheduled job run starts and completes, provided these channels are activated in your user settings.

For detailed guidance on how to subscribe to notifications, please refer to the Notifications documentation.

That’s a wrap! 🎁

Thank you for reading. We hope these updates help make your day-to-day work in SAP PaPM Cloud even smoother. Stay tuned for the next edition of our “What’s New” series!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-03-02
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

As we step into a new month, it’s time to highlight the latest updates and improvements introduced to the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM). In this edition of What’s New, I’ll guide you through the newest enhancements designed to support more efficient modeling, better transparency, and an overall smoother experience.

If you’d like to explore previous updates from the team, feel free to check out all past posts using the PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag.

Model

Enhanced Read Access for Protected Environments

When working in protected environments, users often notice that certain parts of the system are restricted by design. This is intentional - these environments are designed to safeguard sensitive customer data. But it also means users have limited visibility into what’s happening behind the scenes. 

When protection is turned on, several important technical details are hidden, such as: 

  • The full list of functions (active or inactive) in Manage Functions and Show Model 
  • All connections 
  • All fields 
  • Data of any internal functions 
  • The ability to run internal functions

While this protects sensitive information, it can add complexity to troubleshooting for both customers and SAP support - often requiring customer data to be exported and restored into a DEV tenant with an unprotected environment.

To improve the experience, the system was adjusted so that users with MODEL_ALL / MODEL roles  –  even with protection set to Yes – would get read access to:

  • Manage Environments

  • Manage Functions

  • Manage Fields

  • Manage Connections

Note: Sensitive items like model filters and the list of models will stay hidden. 

  • Manage Partitions

This helps users see the list of functions and the basic setup, making issue analysis much easier.

To keep the environment secure, areas that contain detailed function configuration logic remain protected and not accessible. Specifically, this refers to:  

  • Manage Model Entities 
  • Manage Model HANAs 
  • Manage Model References 
  • Manage Views and Manage Model Views 
  • Manage Joins and Manage Model Joins 
  • Manage Connections and Manage Model Connections 
  • Manage Derivations and Manage Model Derivations 
  • Manage Calculations and Manage Model Calculations 
  • Manage Allocations and Manage Model Allocations 
  • Manage Currency Conversions and Manage Model Currency Conversions 
  • Manage Unit Conversions and Manage Model Unit Conversions 
  • Manage Transfer Structures and Manage Model Transfer Structures 
  • Manage Collections and Manage Model Collections 
  • Manage Readers 
  • Manage Writers

And other areas that expose detailed function information.

Even in protected mode, users can still run functions with API-State = ACTIVE through Manage Activities, just as they do today. 

In essence, the updated behavior is designed to create a more effective balance by providing:

  • Greater transparency for troubleshooting
  • Reduced reliance on exporting data to a development tenant 
  • Continued protection of sensitive internal model information

This makes protected environments safer and more usable.

Value Help for Client Field in Conversion Functions

A new enhancement has been introduced to improve how the Client field is maintained in Conversion functions. With this update, the Client field now provides a Value Help option that allows users to select a value instead of typing it manually. 

 The Client value can now be assigned from: 

  • A field available in the input function
  • A parameter defined in the related environment 

This improvement ensures consistent client assignment and helps the system select the correct records from conversion tables based on tenant-specific data.

The enhancement is applied to the following functions: 

  • Currency Conversion 
  • Model Currency Conversion 
  • Unit Conversion 
  • Model Unit Conversion 

This change improves accuracy, reduces manual effort, and ensures consistent behavior across all conversion processes. 

That wraps up this edition!

We hope you enjoy exploring the latest features and improvements delivered in SAP PaPM Cloud UM. Don’t forget to check back next month for more updates.

Thank you for taking the time to read, and feel free to share this post with your network.

entry.title: Customizing Remote Function Adapter's Component List for SAP PAPM On Premise
entry.content:
Profitability and Performance Management (PAPM) offers a list of Remote Function Adapter (RFA) types which can be used to communicate to other applications and systems. This is done by reading or by posting data, on the same system as PAPM, or on remote systems (for example, SAP ERP or SAP S/4HANA). 

Each RFA type comes with a predefined list of fields, show as components on the function's Rules Tab. To these components, the user can map fields which provide input values or store output results. The RFA works as a wrapper, by calling SAP BAPIs, custom function modules or OData services, and mapping its list of components to the corresponding importing and exporting parameters. 

Until now, to extend a function's list of components in order to meet the specific needs of a user, the user would create a new feature request here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3041.

To address the users' demands for flexibility and efficiency, I will guide you on how to customise the list of RFA components, and add new fields to it. 

1. Identifying the SAP function module called by the RFA function. 

Use the table below to find which BAPI is called by your RFA type. 

RFA typeTechnical abbreviationSAP Function ModuleNote
Finance Accounts PayableFI_APBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance Accounts Payable ItemsFI_APIBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance Accounts ReceivableFI_ARBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance Accounts Receivable ItemsFI_ARIBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance ExtendedFI_EXTBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance General LedgerFI_GLBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance General Ledger ItemsFI_GLIBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Purchase OrderPOBAPI_PO_CREATE1Limitation: systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower - see details below
Primary Cost RepostingPRBAPI_ACC_PRIMARY_COSTS_POSTLimitation: systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower - see details below

Sales and Distribution

type = 'SALESORDER'

type = 'QUOTATION'

type = 'INQUIRY'

SD

 

BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2

BAPI_QUOTATION_CREATEFROMDATA2

BAPI_INQUIRY_CREATEFROMDATA2

Limitation: systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower - see details below
Secondary Cost Element PostingSPBAPI_ACC_MANUAL_ALLOC_POST 

 

2. Identifying the function module parameter and field you need

Let's have a simple example, where the user wants to add the field 'Company ID of Trading Partner' to RFA FI_AP. 

  • On your target system, run transaction SE37.
  • Fill in function module name - BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST and click the Display button (Figure 1). 
  • Go to tab called Tables (Figure 2).
  • Double click on the associated type BAPIACGL09, corresponding to parameter name ACCOUNTGL.
  • Identify the field TRADE_ID, which has the type CHAR 6, decimals 0, and the description 'Company ID of Trading Partner' (Figure 3). Copy these field technical details.

 Figure 1

Figure 2

Figure 3

3. Maintaining the RFA customizing view

Before modifying the customizing view, we need to check that the necessary placeholder for our field already exists. This is a mandatory condition for the end user to independently add their desired fields.

A. Identifying the placeholder

  • On your local system, run transaction /N/NXI/P1_MF.
  • Navigate in the tree to 'Remote Function Placeholder'.
  • In the list, search for the Placeholder which has the same exact name as the BAPI parameter you just viewed. For our current example, this is 'ACCOUNTGL' (Figure 4).

 Figure 4

Once we make sure that we have all the necessary information, we can move forward to the final step.

B. Adding the new RFA component.

  • Navigate in the tree to 'Remote Function Adapter Mapping'. 
  • Fill in Remote Function Adapter Type with the value 'FI_AP' (code for Finance Accounts Payable), then press 'Enter'.
  • Press the 'Change' button (or Ctrl+F1) and then the 'New Entries' button (or F5).
  • Add all the necessary technical information for the field. (Figure 5)
    Component: structure_name-field_name. Each field belongs to a group of fields, which are then mapped to a BAPI structure. It helps to follow the examples of the fields already defined.
    Sequence: the order in which the components appear on the application UI, on the RFA Rules tab.
    Placeholder: must have the same technical name as the BAPI's parameter.
    Is Mandatory: when choosing Yes, the component will be colored on the application UI. It will red, when the field is not mapped to a value or an environment field, and green, once the field is mapped.
    Declaration Type: RFA components which will be passed to processing are of type Input. RFA components which represent results of processing are of type Output. 
    DDIC Data Type, Data Length, Data Decimals and Name are all technical information taken from the BAPI field definition. 
  • Press 'Save' and select an appropriate Transport Request.

 Figure 5

After activating the objects and releasing the transport requests, the newly added field can be found on the application UI.

❗Limitations

Please note this user-led customization process has the following limitations:

  1. Only available from some RFAs (listed in the table above), where the functionality is simple enough that it can easily align with the customization. 
  2. Only available for systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower, in case of RFA PO, PR, SD. PAPM is optimized for S/4HANA 1610. If S/4HANA Version is higher than 1610, the parameters of SAP function modules may have changed data types. This would require more complex work in order to add new RFA components, not just performing the above steps. 
  3. Only available for fields of BAPI parameters which can already be found in the Placeholders list. Adding a new placeholder would require more complex work, including source code changes.

If you find that you are facing one of the above limitations, please create a new feature request for the enhancement you wish to acquire, here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3041.

 

By following these steps - identifying the SAP function module name, identifying the parameter name and field technical details, maintaining the RFA's customizing view, you now have everything you need to complete the process from start to finish. Once you implement this guide, you'll save time, gain agency and get better and faster results every time you repeat the process. Start with a field and carefully test the results of your work. 

If you run into questions, drop them in the comments and I am happy to help!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud UM Modeling Performance Improvement Tips and Tricks
entry.content:
SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) empowers users to design content‑based applications that efficiently handle high‑volume data calculations and complex processing requirements. By leveraging its flexible and scalable modeling capabilities, SAP PaPM Cloud UM allows organizations to transform large datasets into actionable insights while maintaining performance and reliability.

When it comes to performance, it’s no longer just a nice-to-have but it has become an absolute necessity. In SAP PaPM Cloud UM, achieving faster calculation runs is a key factor in ensuring efficient memory usage, enhancing user experience, and allowing for better data handling as business data continues to grow over time.

So, with all that in mind, this blog post will walk you through some of the performance improvement tips and tricks for SAP PaPM Cloud UM based on existing documentation, smarter data model design, making the most out of parallelization.

To clearly outline the key areas of performance optimization, I’ve organized the discussion into sections. This approach also allows you to easily navigate to the topics most relevant to your specific use case.

  1. General Performance Recommendations
  2. Capacity Sizing
  3. Parallelization
  4. Modeling Optimization
  5. Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

Let us proceed.

I.        General Performance Recommendations

  • HANA SDA is a compelling synchronous remote access feature, but because of that nature, reading or writing fails immediately if the communication with the source system is disrupted for a microsecond, the remote system is down for maintenance, some authorization tokens get expired or access control changes. Therefore, it should be used only in exceptional cases.

    When reading from HANA SDA (Smart Data Access) sources, consider the following: Create statistics for an SDA Virtual table as described in Create Statistics on a Virtual Table or Linked Database | SAP Help Portal.

  • If the Manage Event Logs application has too many entries, you may consider performing event log archiving and deletion. For more information, see Manage Archives.

    You can also maximize archiving via schedule by following the steps described in SAP PaPM Cloud UM’s Event Log Archive and Deletion using SAP Job Scheduling Service.

  • Verify whether the HANA DB parameters have been set correctly as described in SAP Note 2186744. If you have set the STATEMENT_MEMORY_LIMIT parameter in the system, the recommended value is 30% of the global allocation limit (if you have 1 HANA node of 3TB, then it should be at 30% of 3TB, which is approximately 1TB).

  • Use the Manage Event Logs application to analyze the runtime performance KPIs for your process. In the detail screen of the Event Log, you can find information about the time spent on input reading, business processing, and writing to the database for each involved function. You can also find information on the number of processed entries and the allocated memory.

    For more information, see Manage Event Logs.

II.        Capacity Sizing

  • Sizing: Incorrect sizing could also cause memory issues. Perform sizing exercises at different points throughout the project (blueprint, realization, final preparation, go-live, change requests/functional changes post go-live, etc.) and not just once during initial phases. Incorrect sizing would also lead to out-of-memory/performance issues (despite performance tuning of the model) because the available hardware resources would not be enough to run the calculations with a high volume of data. You could consider engaging Expert Sizing Consulting.

  • Before going to production, perform a performance test such as a load/scalability/volume test on a preproduction system. You can use incremental volumes up to production-like volumes to see if the configured model scales linearly, and to get an idea of how much time/memory it will take. This is necessary to ensure that you have performed all necessary performance tuning in your configured calculation model and to confirm whether the sizing of the production system is correct. In such tests, you might realize that the initial/theoretical sizing numbers are not applicable anymore, for example, in case the scenarios/data models/assumptions have changed. As a result, you may need a larger or smaller-sized production system based on the current realizations.

III.        Parallelization

  • The Manage Partitions application allows you to manage large datasets efficiently, optimize performance, and maintain data integrity to maximize your data management capabilities. In the Universal Model application, partitioning is the process of dividing a large dataset into smaller and more manageable pieces of information. This way, the application not only enhances the speed of data processing but also allows for better utilization of system resources.

    For more information, see Manage Partitions and SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model: What’s New as of 2024-10-07?

  • If your calculation model/environment involves writing high-volume data to many model tables and you have implemented packaging using the environment partitioning setting as mentioned in the previous step, consider partitioning these model tables. For SAP PaPM-generated tables (of Model Entity function or any other function's buffer result table), this can be done by maintaining the partitioning ID in the Writer, Partition settings and reactivating the function.

    This will improve the write/insert times considerably and thereby enhance the overall processing time.

  • Apply hash partitioning or, if required, a combined hash-range partitioning on all Writer functions. A suitable number of partitions is 100. In case the Writer writes into a Model Entity, apply the partitioning on the Model Entity. This will reduce the time to write data to database roughly by a factor of 2.

  • When using a Process, always assign a Writer as the "To Activity". With this setup, you can make use of applied hash partitioning to have a significant improvement in terms of performance since you cannot apply Hash Partitioning to other functions, for example Join function.

IV.        Modeling Optimization

  • Avoid unnecessary functions or reduce staging functions in between to reduce materialization of data, which will consume significant memory and lead to longer runtime. In addition, you can combine functions to achieve calculations on the already/same materialized data. Effectively try to reduce the number of functions in the overall environment/calculation model.

  • Maintain filters/selections as early as possible, and apply selections already in the Input tab, if possible, rather than in rules.

  • Reduce the number of columns/fields if they are not really needed in the data you process as early as possible, for example, in cases where most columns are not actually needed during the calculation but were just included for reporting purposes.

  • When modeling a calculation model in SAP PaPM Cloud UM based on previous/legacy solutions, avoid taking over the logic one-to-one. Instead, reduce the number of functions by using SAP PaPM Cloud UM standard functionalities.

  • Use iterative loops with caution and careful consideration (if there are no other SAP PaPM Cloud UM standard options available). Functions or chains of functions that are run within iterative loops should be limited as much as possible.

  • Avoid using nested iterative loops, if possible. Be aware that using nested iterative loops would cost significant time and memory, and the data would grow significantly over every iteration, sometimes exponentially if not carefully configured.

  • To run a function and its related input functions without saving results, set a condition that is never met. This approach saves writing time, especially with high-volume data. The system runs all functions in the input network, but the condition prevents function from selecting any entries, so no data is written to the database.

  • If possible, aggregate data, and reduce granularity as early as possible in the calculation model. This will reduce the number of records to be processed by the calculation model. Verify whether the business scenario/requirement really needs the detailed granularity level of data to be processed throughout the calculation model.

  • For allocation scenarios, check and compare the approach of an account-based allocation vs. a costing/value field-based allocation approach. In certain scenarios, a costing/value field-based allocation approach is more performant/feasible.

  • In an Allocation function, use allocation type “Allocation with detailed offset records” judiciously, and only if needed, as this type produces much more granular results than the other types, resulting in a greater number of records.

V.        Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

1.     Are partitioning settings implicitly applied to all upstream functions leading up to a Writer function that has partitioning configured?

Yes, Hash-Range Partitioning supports the run of the Writer function, including its upstream function chain in sequential levels of parallel range-packages, for example, to save HANA memory or to reduce/control the overall HANA workload, that is, partially sequential or leveled package parallelization.

2.     If partitioning is applied in the Writer function with an Allocation function as input, shall partitioning be applied to the Sender or Receiver input?

If you use Hash-Dynamic or Hash-Range partitioning on the underlying input which is your Model Entity, the reading speed can be improved. If this type of partitioning is applied to the Sender input, then only the Sender reading is improved. If the same partitioning is applied to the Receiver input, then Receiver reading is also improved.

3.    Will an Information function also apply “partitioning”, meaning the SQL statement will be run separately for each partition and then assembled in memory?

Yes, a separate SQL statement is processed per partition. These partitions process different records.

4.    Does partitioning require larger memory utilization for Information functions since it will have a separate SQL statement run for each partition?

Yes, running multiple partitions consumes larger memory utilization in exchange for faster processing time.

That’s all for now! Thank you for taking the time to read the Modeling Performance Improvement Tips and Tricks of SAP PaPM Cloud UM. I hope the insights shared help you enhance and optimize your SAP PaPM Cloud UM.

See you again in the next blog post!
